problem_statement:
TEST Add pytest marker for narwhals
# Description

This might look like an odd request, but please bear with me.
In Narwhals we take some effort to make sure that what we release/merge does not end up breaking downstream libraries that use Narwhals. We run tests of these libraries as part of our usual CI with Narwhals installing from the PR codebase (see [downstream_tests.yaml](https://github.com/narwhals-dev/narwhals/blob/main/.github/workflows/downstream_tests.yml)).

For some libraries, these test might take a while and we end up running only specific tests (see [darts example](https://github.com/narwhals-dev/narwhals/blob/63c8e4771a1df4e0bfeea5559c303a4a447d5cc2/.github/workflows/downstream_tests.yml#L534-L536)).
In a way this is a hack which might break easily in (at least) the following two scenarios:

- A test is moved -> breaks our CI (happened with plotly)
- A new test is added and we don't run it -> silent "failure"

I would consider Fairlearn to be among the libraries which tests can be quite long in their entirety. 

---

In light of the progressive Narwhals adoption in Fairlearn, I am here to ask what should we test, that also runs in reasonable time.

Would the minimal-deps test suffice? Will there be in the future parts of the code outside of what's tested with minimal-deps workflow that use Narwhals, either directly or indirectly?

One idea would be to add a `pytestmark = pytest.mark.narwhals` at the top of the test files that run with Narwhals.
In this way we simply run `pytest test -m narwhals` to collect all and only these tests, without the need to change it over time.

Let me know if this is a viable idea.

---

Narwhals issue to track it: [narwhals#2603](https://github.com/narwhals-dev/narwhals/issues/2603)
hints_text:
> I would consider Fairlearn to be among the libraries which tests can be quite long in their entirety.

Agreed  💯 (I'm working on speeding them up 🤞)

> One idea would be to add a pytestmark

I'm ok with this solution, it would resolve the first breaking case you mentionned (a test is moved), and the second one too (silent failures) provided that we do not forget to mark new narwhals related tests 👍 

@fairlearn/fairlearn-maintainers Any objections ? I guess that in practice this would involve:
- registring the marker in our [pytest config](https://github.com/fairlearn/fairlearn/blob/main/pyproject.toml#L18).
- marking some files as @FBruzzesi suggests, (or some specific tests with `@pytest.mark.narwhals` at the top)
I am also in favour of this, it makes sense for both sides. Maybe we can do a check if a test is added/changed to have a tag like we have for the changelog so we don't forget? "not a narwhals test"

problem_statement:
MNT Make fairlearn scipy 1.16.0 compatible
`Scipy` has a new version, 1.16.0. [It introduces some changes] (https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/release/1.16.0-notes.html)that require updating of our codebase.

Some of the issues are [these future deprecations](https://github.com/fairlearn/fairlearn/blob/main/pyproject.toml#L27)(added by @parulgupta1004 to our config, to be removed when a more permanent solution is found), but also other issues. 

Whoever takes the issue should remember to remove the limit pointing to version `< 1.16.0` in the `requirements.txt`.
hints_text: We will also need to remove the scipy version upper bound on the xgboost config yaml here https://github.com/fairlearn/fairlearn/blob/main/test_othermlpackages%2Fconda-xgboost.yaml#L11
all_hints_text: We will also need to remove the scipy version upper bound on the xgboost config yaml here https://github.com/fairlearn/fairlearn/blob/main/test_othermlpackages%2Fconda-xgboost.yaml#L11

problem_statement:
MNT Discontinue support for Python 3.9, add Python 3.13
Because of EOL for Python 3.9 soon, we can remove the support for the version. The nightly has been testing for Python 3.13 for months now and we can safely add it. A nightly testing for the next version should be added too.
